message.content:
# How DNS Resolves a Domain Name to an IP Address

## Overview

The Domain Name System (DNS) translates human-readable domain names (e.g., `www.example.com`) into machine-readable IP addresses (e.g., `93.184.217.42`). This process involves a hierarchy of servers and can take several steps.

---

## The DNS Hierarchy

```
Root Servers (.)
    └── Top-Level Domain (TLD) Servers (.com, .org, .net, etc.)
            └── Authoritative Name Servers (example.com)
                    └── Final IP Address
```

---

## Step-by-Step Resolution Process

### Step 1: User Requests a Domain Name

A user types `www.example.com` into their browser. The browser checks its **local cache** for a recent record. If found, it uses it immediately. If not, the process begins.

### Step 2: Operating System & Local Resolver

The operating system checks its own cache (e.g., `/etc/hosts` on Linux/macOS or the DNS cache on Windows). If still not found, it sends the query to the **Recursive Resolver** (also called the DNS resolver or stub resolver).

> **Recursive Resolver**: Typically provided by your ISP or a public service like Google DNS (`8.8.8.8`) or Cloudflare (`1.1.1.1`). It is responsible for performing the full lookup on behalf of the client.

### Step 3: Recursive Resolver Queries the Root Server

The recursive resolver contacts one of the **13 logical Root Servers** (with many physical instances worldwide). The root server does **not** know the IP address of `www.example.com`, but it knows which **TLD server** handles `.com` domains.

It responds
